coredata.dc:title: Room temperature manufacturing photoluminescent graphene quantum dots based on MXene
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, E.Joo
Jeon, Ok Sung
Yang, Jong Uk
Shin, Min Kyoon
Yoo, Young Joon
Park, Sang Yoon
coredata.dc:description: Carbide–derived graphene quantum dot (GQDs) have high potential to the photoluminescent field owing to its non-toxicity, strong light harvesting capability and large surface area. In order to cost-effectively and stably produce GQDs, lowering synthesis temperature of GQDs is one of the great candidates, but it has been still a big challenge. Herein, the photoluminescent GQDs were obtained by KOH (aq) of MXene at room temperature controlling the reaction time and concentration. This facile and cost-effective method can produce GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles which had the average particle size about 1.5 nm with minor portion of amorphous carbon. The functionalized and defects in GQDs/TiO2 crystalline nanoparticles were characterized according to fabricating conditions and photoluminescent properties were precisely detected. Although the quantum yield of fabricated GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles is 2.06%, it could be improved by optimizing the conditions of KOH activation. It was the meaningful result using facile synthesis for GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles at room temperature and it could encompass key factor to manufacturing quantum dots. The technique can be considered general enough to expand to various research fields such as information security, display, biocompatible multicolor cellular imaging and sensors.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles at room temperature and 50 °C

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic facile fabrication process of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles at room temperature. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images of (a) MAX, (b) MXene, (c) the O element concentration according to diverse concentrations of KOH during activation of MXene and (d) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image, (b) selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern and (c) the particle size distribution of GQD/TiO2 nanoparticles measured by TEM results. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of MAX, Mxene, GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, (b) FT–IR spectra of MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, and (c) Raman shift results of MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of (a) C 1s and (b) Ti 2p elements in GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, and the photoluminescence spectra of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles according to (b) the KOH concentration and (c) temperature, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Photographs of lettered GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles deposited on glass substrate under (a) visible light and (b) 365 nm UV light.

head.ce:title: Room temperature manufacturing photoluminescent graphene quantum dots based on MXene

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Applied Electromagnetic Research (CAER), Advanced Institutes of Convergence Technology (AICT), Seoul National University, Suwon–si, Gyeonggi–do, 16229, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Applied Electromagnetic Research (CAER), Advanced Institutes of Convergence Technology (AICT), Seoul National University, Suwon-si, Gyeonggi-do, 16229, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbide–derived graphene quantum dot (GQDs) have high potential to the photoluminescent field owing to its non-toxicity, strong light harvesting capability and large surface area. In order to cost-effectively and stably produce GQDs, lowering synthesis temperature of GQDs is one of the great candidates, but it has been still a big challenge. Herein, the photoluminescent GQDs were obtained by KOH (aq) of MXene at room temperature controlling the reaction time and concentration. This facile and cost-effective method can produce GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles which had the average particle size about 1.5 nm with minor portion of amorphous carbon. The functionalized and defects in GQDs/TiO2 crystalline nanoparticles were characterized according to fabricating conditions and photoluminescent properties were precisely detected. Although the quantum yield of fabricated GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles is 2.06%, it could be improved by optimizing the conditions of KOH activation. It was the meaningful result using facile synthesis for GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles at room temperature and it could encompass key factor to manufacturing quantum dots. The technique can be considered general enough to expand to various research fields such as information security, display, biocompatible multicolor cellular imaging and sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dot (QD) has been gaining significant momentum in the photoluminescent field because of its band gap tunability, strong light harvesting capability and large surface to volume ratios [1–6]. 0D photoluminescent (PL) QDs could be prepared by chopping the 2D transition metal carbide nanosheets to smaller than 10 nm, due to the quantum confinement and edge effect [7,8]. Typically, cadmium based QDs are the most intensively studied nanostructured materials because they have superior quantum efficiency and color tunability which lead to have attractive attention as a display material [9], but their toxicity limit significantly direct use in biological and optical applications [10]. Among all the nanoparticles, graphene QDs (GQDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles are promising candidates to replace cadmium based QDs because these exhibit great prospects for applications to their non-toxicity and photoluminescence. Furthermore, cost–effective manufacturing process led to prefer GQD and TiO2 nanoparticles as an alternative [11–19].

The fabrication methods of the GQDs can be classified into two categories, top–down strategy and bottom-up strategy. Due to its simplicity and effectiveness, the top-down strategy is extensive method to synthesize GQDs which is cutting the graphitic carbon-based precursor. Among diverse top-down methods, the hydrothermal method is a simple and rapid method. GQDs can be gained from carbon materials under the conditions of high temperature and high pressure in the process. The GQDs were generally formed with facile method under heat treatment above 150 °C [20,21]. Pan et al. reported that the hydrothermal reaction started under alkaline conditions for 10 h at 200 °C and then, 5–13 nm sized GQDs were gained [22]. Tetsuka et al. presented that heat treated GO and ammonia solution formed GQDs at 150 °C for 5 h in an autoclave [23]. Heating temperature in synthesis procedure is usually important factor to influence the optical properties, so researchers have paid large attention to demonstrate how the temperature affects the properties of QDs. In the case of nanomaterials such as QDs, lowering the temperature during fabrication process is significant to obtain phase stability and avoid agglomeration of nanoparticles [24,25]. Qi Xue et al. investigated the influence of the reaction temperatures including 100, 120, and 150 °C on the average size of QDs. It demonstrated that QDs synthesized about 100 °C (low temperature) had relatively high quantum yields [26]. Furthermore, large-scale production of nanoparticles with high temperature still difficult and expensive [27]. The synthesis temperature is usually determining the size, cost-effectiveness, and quantum yields of the QDs [28]. For these reasons, reducing the temperature of facile synthesis procedure is necessary. In order to reduce fabricating temperature, though Wang et al. presented new method to form GQD at room temperature using electron–beam irradiation, it was still challenging to be facile and cost–effective method [29].

To solve these issues, we attempted to synthesize GQDs using metal carbide as precursor in this study. Atomic–level control of carbon structure can be generally and simply derived from 3D structured metal carbide. Naguib et al. introduced novel synthesis method of 2D transition metal carbide inherited from MAX phases with a formula of M2C, M3C2 or M4C3 (M = the early transition metal, X = N, C or CN) so–called MXene, Mn+1XnTx (n = 1, 2, or 3, Tx = –OH, –O or –F) [30–32]. Ti3C2Tx is typical structure of MXene which is synthesized via selectively etching the Al layer from Ti3AlC2 [33–37]. The synthesis and applications of novel 2D materials recently have limelight in science and technology [38–41]. In these research trend of MXene, solvothermal strategy was also found to be a facile and universal method to generate QD from MXene nanosheet [42,43]. Unfortunately, in order to form quantum dot phase arisen from MXene, conventional synthesis procedures were also composed of thermal treatment above 150 °C as mentioned above [44–49].

In this respect, herein, we introduced novel and facile synthesis procedure to successfully fabricate GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles with slight amounts of amorphous carbon at room temperature with KOH as an adjuvant in this study. The phase stability and photoluminescence of the GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles also showed significant dependence on the KOH concentration in solvent and adequate reaction time. The effects of various KOH concentration and reaction time on the characterization of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles and photoluminescent properties were studied.

In order to separate MXene layer from MAX, MAX powder was gradually dispersed into the bottle filled with HF, ultrasonificated and retained for 3 days. The dispersion was first filtrated through the 0.1 μm membrane and the filtrate was collected as MXene. Washing as–prepared MXene with D.I. water and ethanol is performed to remove HF and impurities from the solution. Filtrated MXene was dried at 40 °C oven overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles at room temperature and 50 °C
ce:section.ce:para: KOH solution was prepared with respective concentrations, 0.1 M, 2 M, 6 M and 10 M. 30 mL various concentrations of KOH solution were blended with 0.25 g MXene and then, vertically rotated with 30 rpm at room temperature or 50 °C overnight. MXene suspension was vacuum-filtrated through Nylon membrane filter (0.2 μm pore size). In order to remove K+ ion, the solution was dialyzed against H2O with an ion-selective dialysis membrane (Spectrum Labs), molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) = 1K) for 3 days. Dialyzed solution, so-called solution of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles was converted to black powder by thermal evaporation. The average particle size of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles in this study was about 1 nm.

The crystal structures of MAX, MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, SmartLab) with Cu radiation at a wavelength of 9 kW. Raman spectroscopy (WITEC Alpha 300 spectrometer) was measured at room temperature under ambient condition with an Ar-ion laser beam at an exciting radiation wavelength of 532 nm on the MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, respectively. In order to detect the bonding states in MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker, Alpha). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE–SEM, Hitachi, S–4800) was used to observe the morphology of samples and Energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) images were performed to identify the dispersion and weight percentage of elements in samples. The oxidation state and atomic ratio of Ti, O and C elements in GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles fabricated by various concentrations of KOH solution were examined by X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG microtech, ESCA2000) which were conducted equipped with an Al Kα source (1486.6 eV). High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR–TEM, JEOL/CEOS, JEM-2100F) was used to distinguish the particle size and crystal structure of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. The particle size distribution of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles was evaluated by TEM results, dynamic light scattering (DLS, Nanotrac) and atomic force microscopy (AFM).

Ultraviolet–visible spectra and photoluminescent properties of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were measured by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV, Biotek, Epoch) and photoluminescence measurement (PL, Scinco, FS–2).

The scheme of the facile synthesis method of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 1 . In general, layered MXene was produced from MAX precursor being large family of ternary carbides with more than 70 members. M–A bonds in MAX are relatively weaker than M–X bonds. HF treatment, facile and effective approach, led to extract ‘A’ layer from the MAX phase along with ultrasonication to promote the exfoliation to 2D MXene [50]. SEM images showed that layered 2D MXene was produced from bulk structure of MAX particles (Fig. 2 a and b). It was confirmed that the Ti and C elements in MXene were well-dispersed on MXene sheet without any aggregation (Fig. S1). Then, from this 2D MXene, GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles can be easily synthesized with KOH at room temperature due to occurrence of highly active reaction. In order to separate and obtain pure GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, the residual MXene was filtered and aqueous KOH was dialyzed using 2 K Da. The reaction time with KOH in solvent and KOH concentration are key factors to optimize the size of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. When it had insufficient dialysis, indicating that KOH reaction was still conducted at the samples, the size of overreacted and aggregated GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles in solution was about ∼1 μm (Fig. S2). It was hard to be determined as nanoparticles. KOH was already used as chemical activator which has potential to form micropore in 2D carbon based nanomaterials [51]. Due to KOH assisting to break Ti–C bonding in MXene and having temper of Ti to be oxidized [52], pore forming led to dissociate 2D structured MXene to be nanoparticles (Fig. S3). In previous research, Na Eun Lee et al. reported that GQD can easily fabricated by silicon etching from silicon carbide based plate [53]. It might applied to similar carbide material, MXene. Etched nanoparticles composed of C element tended to be GQDs and the slight portion of amorphous carbon. It was already reported that GQDs are highly stable and hydrophilic owing to the abundant oxidation sites and hydroxyl, carboxylic groups on their surface and edges [54]. On the other hand, Ti elements separated from MXene in solution were easily and chemically oxidized by alkoxide [55], so these etched Ti based nanoparticles altered to TiO2. In order to additionally probe the status of Ti component after KOH activation, the overactivation with KOH solution for long time generated white aggregated particles and it was analyzed. The results indicated that highly crystalline and aggregated TiO2 particles with irregular shape were fabricated (Fig. S4, Fig. S5 and Table S1). KOH concentration also can be important factor to form adequate size of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. According to increment in KOH concentration, GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles in solvent were dissolved from MXene (Fig. S6) and the contents of O element in GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were drastically increased to approximately 80.5% at 10 M KOH (Fig. 2c). When the concentration of KOH was increased, severe chemical activity induced to form excessive micropore and tune their morphology as dots. O element concentration in samples was corresponded to formation of surface defects in GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. When it was fabricated via this precise condition, though the synthesis temperature was considerably low as room temperature, GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were successfully fabricated (Fig. 2d). The characterization of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles was shown in Fig. 3 . The crystalline structure of GQD in GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles was investigated by TEM image and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 3a and b). According to HR-TEM images, the lattice spacing between adjacent lattice planes corresponds to the d-spacing of the (100) planes and is 0.24 nm (Fig. S7) [56]. It was important that sp2 hybridization of GQDs led to obtain actual lattice distance as about 0.24 nm though lattice parameter of amorphous carbon QDs is slightly longer about 0.34 nm due to large amounts of sp3 hybridized [57]. It referred to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene which was easily formed. The minor portion of nanoparticles were amorphous carbon without any lattices (Fig. S8). The size distribution of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were measured by TEM results with the size of 50 particles shown in Fig. 3c. It was evenly distributed in the average size of about 1.5 nm with the range of 1 nm–2 nm. These results were well corresponded to DLS data (Fig. S9). The average height of particles in samples, about 1.5 nm, was also detected by atomic force microscopy (AFM) data (Fig. S10). In Fig. S11 and S12, (101) lattice plane of TiO2 with the length of 0.35 nm was also detected and distributed in the samples.

In order to identify crystalline structure specifically, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of MAX, MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were measured (Fig. 4 a). The (002) peak of MXene was broadened and shifted towards smaller angle compared to that of MAX. It means occurrence of successful Al atom extraction from Ti3AlC2 MXene [50]. Furthermore, vanishment of peak at 39° indicated that fabricated MXene has pure phase [58]. The size of nanoparticles contributed strongly to the broadening of XRD pattern. In XRD pattern of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, shifting inclination of (002) plane peak was not detected and significantly broadened due to its particle size. Main broad peak of GQD was centered at 25° which is attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms [59].

The specific types of chemical bonding in MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles had been investigated by FT–IR (Fig. 4b). FT–IR spectra were comparable to MXene that the absorption bands at O–H (1390 cm−1 and 3420 cm−1), CO (1650 cm−1), C–O (1100 cm−1) and Ti–O (630 cm−1) can be assigned to functional group on the surface after KOH treatment at room temperature in FT–IR spectra (Fig. 4b) [43–48].

The Raman spectra data of MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 4c. Though impurities in MXene led to disturb detecting Raman shift values at D and G bands arisen from graphitic carbon structure, D and G bands located at the 1348 cm−1 and 1564 cm−1 in GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, respectively [44,49]. These chemical states of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were additionally investigated by X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As mentioned above, GQD had sufficient functionalized bonding in carbon group. XPS spectra in the C1s region revealed the existence of functionalized carboxylic acid groups and C–O, CO bonding (Fig. 5 a) [60]. The MXene oxidation resulted in the formation of TiO2, GQDs and its networks [61]. Furthermore, due to extreme reaction of MXene with strongly alkaline solution, it concluded to completely break the Ti–C bonding and form GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. Though Ti–C bonding in C1s XPS spectra was located at ∼281 eV in formal case of MXene, it was not detected in this study. As shown in Fig. 5b, main distinct deconvoluted peaks of Ti 2p XPS corresponded to TiO2 2p doublet pair orbitals. Furthermore, groups of Ti–C bonding in samples were tidily vanished, located at ∼456.2 eV for the C–Ti3+ 2p3/2 and ∼455.21 eV for the C–Ti2+ 2p3/2 [62]. It indicated that Ti composites in MXene were entirely and successfully converted to the GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles.

The graphene core part and functional group in GQDs determined intrinsic emission and surface state, respectively [63]. The bandgap of the conjugated π-domains is the intrinsic PL center. When quantum dots are smaller than their exciton Bohr radius, the quantum confinement effect (QCE) occurred at GQDs [64]. GQD is composed of light carbon based atoms which has low dielectric constant and weak spin-orbit interaction [65]. These have relatively large energy band via strong carrier-carrier interactions and electronic states with spin multiplicity which led to have dominance on fluorescence. GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles via KOH treatment were approximately 1.5 mm diameter which were small enough. The optical properties of the samples were then examined by UV–vis and PL measurements. In order to determine excitation wavelength in PL measurement, it is preferred to measure the UV absorption peak to figure out the suitable excitation range. Fig. 6 a shows the UV–Vis spectra of the MXene and GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. From the UV–vis spectrum of the MXene, the band gap energy of oxidized MXene exhibited peak at 225 nm assigned to the π–π∗ transition of graphitic sp2 domains [66]. In solution of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles, π–π∗ transitions of electrons in π-conjugated sp2 CC bonds presented in the GQDs contributed to broadly absorb the UV around 230 nm, and additional n-π∗ electronic transitions in non-bonding orbitals from the functional groups attached to sp2 hybridized carbon at 314 nm was also observed [67]. Though the UV–vis spectrum of TiO2 and MXene exhibited that absorption did not occur in the region of above 400 nm wavelength [68,69], the adsorption region of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles certainly increased in visible region and red-shifted. It implied that GQDs provide synergetic effects to TiO2 as extension of the photoresponse range of the TiO2 to visible and near infrared light. Furthermore, nano-crystallized TiO2 absorbed at 350 nm reacted with intense UV light and acted as photocatalyst through its hydroxyl radical which led to cut off the covalent bonds [70]. These changes in the energy band gap abiding by the size and surface state of nanoparticles from KOH activation may contribute to different PL properties.

Fig. 6b and c presented the photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles during fabrication process with KOH concentration and temperature. The highest intensity of PL of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles was observed at 400 nm when excited at 320 nm. (Fig. 6b). Intensities of emission spectra were increased as the KOH concentration was increased from 0.1 M to 10 M, and it drastically soared at 10 M. It was corresponded to the ratio of surface defect formation as shown in Fig. 2c. As known in general, excess oxygen-containing quantum dots can facilitate non-radiative recombination emission which led to have low quantum yield [71]. The upward tendency in PL intensity according to the increment of KOH concentration implied that oxygen contents in GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles do not negatively influence on quantum yield by forming functional groups over GQDs or surface defects in GQDs. Fig. 6c illustrates the change in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles at room temperature and 50 °C. Interestingly, the stronger emission peak of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles appears at room temperature than 50 °C. Additional heating process with the same condition of established synthesis method of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles at room temperature might insert negative effects such as overreaction to nanoparticles. It referred that optimizing the condition of fabricating process was important and preferred to obtain high quantum yield. In this study, we obtained 2.06% of quantum yield which can be improved by optimizing KOH conditions.

Photographs of word were depicted under visible light and 365 nm UV light of the GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles deposited on the glass substrate are shown in Fig. 7 a and b, respectively. Various shapes of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were shown in Fig. S13. It implied that it was possible to achieve the information hiding, information security and anticounterfeiting as various applications with these nanoparticles using UV light.

In this work, a novel and facile room temperature-based approach has been established to fabricated GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles with minor portion of amorphous carbon from MXene controlling various conditions of KOH solution. The simply synthesized GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles were demonstrated by crystalline structure and detection of chemical state which had removal of Ti–C bonding. These QDs exhibited the synthesis condition dependent photoluminescence behavior depending on KOH concentration and temperature. The surface defect formation in GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles attributed to emission spectra according to the KOH concentration, and high emission in room temperature. It can be a good guidance for the economical and facile method for fabrication of GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles.

E.Joo Yang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Ok Sung Jeon: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Data curation. Jong Uk Yang: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Min Kyoon Shin: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Young Joon Yoo: Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft. Sang Yoon Park: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP, Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (ICT) and Future Planning) (Nos. 2016R1A3B1908336, 2016M3A7B4910458, 2017M3C1A9069591 and 2018M3A7B4070990).
